county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Custer County formed 1877 from unorganized territory. County seat is Broken Bow. Large western county. Major homesteading destination in the 1880s-1890s. Parent to Blaine and Logan counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Custer County (formed 1877, seat Broken Bow) is one of Nebraska's larger western counties. Records are at the courthouse in Broken Bow. Custer County was a major homesteading destination -- thousands of homestead entries from the 1880s and 1890s are documented at BLM GLO. The county was parent to several smaller counties (Blaine, Logan). Settlement accelerated after the Burlington Railroad reached the area. The Custer County Historical Society in Broken Bow has local materials. Anglo-American, German, and Scandinavian settlers predominated. The 1890 census was destroyed, making the 1885 state census critical for mid-decade research. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. History Nebraska holds supplementary manuscripts. The Custer County newspaper (Custer County Chief, 1882-) is a valuable local source.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Custer County. County Register of Deeds in Broken Bow holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Dakota County formed 1855 from Burt County. County seat is Dakota City. Northeastern Missouri River county. South Sioux City is part of the Sioux City, Iowa, metropolitan area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Dakota County (formed 1855, seat Dakota City) is a northeastern Missouri River county. Records are at the courthouse in Dakota City. South Sioux City, the county's largest community, sits across the Missouri from Sioux City, Iowa. The county has strong ties to the Sioux City metropolitan area. Meatpacking and agriculture are key industries. Homestead and pre-emption entries at BLM GLO document early settlers. The county has significant Hispanic/Latino population from meatpacking industry recruitment. German and Scandinavian immigrants were early settlers. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The territorial censuses and 1885 state census are available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The Sioux City Public Museum and Sioux City Public Library across the river in Iowa may hold relevant cross-border family materials.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Dakota County. County Register of Deeds in Dakota City holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Dawes County formed 1885 from Sioux County. County seat is Chadron. Northwestern Panhandle county. Borders Pine Ridge Sioux Reservation in South Dakota. Fort Robinson (1874-1948).
county_metadata.research_tips: Dawes County (formed 1885, seat Chadron) is a northwestern Panhandle county. Records are at the courthouse in Chadron. Chadron State College archives hold regional history materials. Dawes County was settled during the final homestead era of the 1880s-1890s. Pine Ridge and the Sioux reservations in South Dakota border the county -- researchers with Lakota/Sioux connections should check NARA Kansas City and the Pine Ridge Agency records. Fort Robinson (1874-1948) was a major military post -- records at NARA and History Nebraska. BLM GLO records document homestead entries. The Dawes County Historical Society preserves local materials. The 1885 state census may have early enumeration. FamilySearch has limited microfilm. The Chadron area attracted ranchers and farmers from across the eastern states.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Dawes County. County Register of Deeds in Chadron holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Dawson County formed 1860 from unorganized territory. County seat is Lexington (formerly Platte City). Central Platte Valley county. Oregon Trail corridor. Fort McPherson (1863-1880).
county_metadata.research_tips: Dawson County (formed 1860, seat Lexington) is a central Platte Valley county. Records are at the courthouse in Lexington (formerly Platte City). The Dawson County Historical Museum in Lexington has extensive local collections. The Platte Valley location made Dawson County a transportation corridor -- the Oregon Trail, Union Pacific Railroad, and Lincoln Highway all passed through. Fort McPherson (1863-1880) military records are at NARA. Homestead entries at BLM GLO are key land records. Swedish settlers were significant in the Lexington area. Meatpacking later attracted Hispanic/Latino workers. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The 1885 state census is available. History Nebraska holds territorial-era and military records. Dawson County newspapers from the 1870s are valuable for genealogical research.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Dawson County. County Register of Deeds in Lexington holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Deuel County formed 1888 from Cheyenne County. County seat is Chappell. Western Panhandle county in the South Platte Valley. Union Pacific Railroad settlement. Parent to Garden County (1909).
county_metadata.research_tips: Deuel County (formed 1888, seat Chappell) in the western Panhandle was carved from Cheyenne County. Records are at the courthouse in Chappell. Settlement was driven by homesteading and the Union Pacific Railroad. Deuel County was parent to Garden County (1909). BLM GLO records document homestead and Timber Culture Act entries. The county sits in the South Platte Valley. German, Scandinavian, and Anglo-American settlers predominated. FamilySearch has limited microfilm. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The 1885 state census may cover this area under Cheyenne County. Big Springs and Chappell newspapers are useful local sources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Deuel County. County Register of Deeds in Chappell holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Dixon County formed 1856 from unorganized territory. County seat is Ponca. Northeast Nebraska. Historical homeland of the Ponca tribe. Standing Bear's 1879 landmark court case connection.
county_metadata.research_tips: Dixon County (formed 1856, seat Ponca) records are at the courthouse in Ponca. Dixon County borders the Ponca tribe's historical homeland. The Ponca tribe was forcibly removed to Indian Territory in 1877 but Chief Standing Bear won the landmark 1879 court case in Omaha establishing that Native Americans are 'persons' under the law. Researchers with Ponca connections should check NARA Kansas City for BIA agency records. Homestead entries at BLM GLO document non-Native settlers. The Dixon County Historical Society preserves local materials. German, Scandinavian, and Anglo-American settlers predominated. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The territorial censuses and 1885 state census are available. History Nebraska holds related materials. The Missouri River bluffs area has early territorial-era settlement history.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Dixon County. County Register of Deeds in Ponca holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Dodge County formed 1854 as original Nebraska Territory county. County seat is Fremont. Major railroad junction town (Union Pacific, Sioux City and Pacific). Territorial-era records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Dodge County (formed 1854, seat Fremont) is one of the original Nebraska Territory counties. Records are at the courthouse in Fremont. The Dodge County Historical Society in Fremont has extensive local collections. Fremont was named for John C. Fremont and became a major railroad junction (Union Pacific, Sioux City and Pacific). Being an original county, Dodge has territorial-era records from 1854. The May Museum in Fremont holds local history. Homestead and pre-emption entries at BLM GLO are key land records. German, Swedish, Danish, and Irish immigrants were numerous. FamilySearch has microfilmed deed, probate, marriage, and court records. The territorial and 1885 state censuses are available. History Nebraska in Lincoln holds supplementary records. Fremont newspapers from the 1860s are valuable for genealogical research.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Dodge County. County Register of Deeds in Fremont holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Douglas County formed 1854 as original Nebraska Territory county. County seat is Omaha. Nebraska's most populous county. Union Pacific Railroad headquarters. Gateway to the West. Major immigrant population.
county_metadata.research_tips: Douglas County (formed 1854, seat Omaha) is Nebraska's most populous county and contains the state's largest city. Records are at the Douglas County Courthouse in Omaha. The Douglas County Register of Deeds has online access to land records. As an original territory county and the territorial/early state capital, Douglas County has the most extensive early records. The Douglas County Historical Society and the Durham Museum (Western Heritage Museum) in Omaha hold major genealogical collections. Omaha was a major railroad hub (Union Pacific headquarters) and gateway to the West. The Trans-Mississippi Exposition (1898) records are at History Nebraska. Omaha's meatpacking district (South Omaha) drew Czech, Polish, Lithuanian, and later Hispanic/Latino workers. African American community records are at the Great Plains Black History Museum. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm. The Omaha Public Library has city directories from the 1860s. Naturalization records filed in Douglas County courts are at History Nebraska and NARA Kansas City.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Douglas County Register of Deeds online access to land records from 1854. BLM GLO patents at glorecords.blm.gov for original federal entries.

source_profiles.source_name: Douglas County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Douglas County Register of Deeds, Omaha
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1854 to present. Nebraska is a public land state; initial federal patents are at BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov). Subsequent transfers recorded at the county Register of Deeds. Includes deeds, mortgages, liens, and plats.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records intact from county formation

source_profiles.source_name: Douglas County Marriage Records -- County Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from 1854. Nebraska marriage licenses issued by county judges (County Court). State registration began 1909. Earlier marriages recorded at county level. FamilySearch has indexed many Nebraska marriages.

source_profiles.source_name: Douglas County Probate and Estate Records -- County Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1854. Nebraska probate is handled by the County Court. Records include wills, letters of administration, estate inventories, guardianship files, and adoption records.

source_profiles.source_name: Douglas County Federal and State Census Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census records for Douglas County (1860-1950). The 1890 census was destroyed by fire. Nebraska conducted a state census in 1885 (available on microfilm). Territorial censuses (1854-1856, 1860) exist for early counties. Available through FamilySearch and Ancestry.
source_profiles.survival_notes: 1890 federal census destroyed nationally; Nebraska 1885 state census survives

source_profiles.source_name: Douglas County Federal Land Patents -- BLM GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Douglas County including homestead entries, pre-emption claims, timber culture entries, cash sales, and military warrant locations. Nebraska is a public land state -- all original land transfers from federal ownership are documented here. Searchable online by name and location.

source_profiles.source_name: Douglas County Cemetery Records -- Find A Grave / Historical Society
source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Douglas County Historical Society / Nebraska State Genealogical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions and burial records for Douglas County. Find A Grave has growing coverage. Township, church, and family cemeteries are common in rural Nebraska. County historical societies and the Nebraska State Genealogical Society have published cemetery transcription volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Douglas County Records on Microfilm -- History Nebraska
source_profiles.coverage_notes: History Nebraska (NSHS) holds microfilm copies of Douglas County records including deed indexes, probate records, marriage records, county board proceedings, and selected court records. Also holds manuscript collections, photographs, and maps related to Douglas County history.

source_profiles.source_name: Douglas County Polish Catholic Church Records
source_profiles.repository: Archdiocese of Omaha / Diocese of Grand Island / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Polish Catholic parish records for Douglas County congregations. Baptisms, confirmations, marriages, and burials. Records often maintained in Latin with Polish surnames. FamilySearch has microfilmed some collections.

source_profiles.source_name: Douglas County Historic Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: History Nebraska / Library of Congress / Newspapers.com
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historic newspapers for Douglas County communities. Obituaries, legal notices, marriage announcements, homestead proof notices, estate settlements, and community news. History Nebraska holds extensive microfilm of Nebraska newspapers. Chronicling America has digitized selections.

source_profiles.source_name: Union Pacific Railroad Land Records -- Douglas County
source_profiles.repository: Union Pacific Historical Collection / History Nebraska / Nebraska State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Union Pacific Railroad land grant and sale records for Douglas County. The UP received alternate sections of land along its right-of-way. Land department records document sales to settlers, town lot sales, and colony arrangements. UP Historical Collection at History Nebraska.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Dundy County formed 1873 from unorganized territory. County seat is Benkelman. Southwestern Nebraska along the Republican River. Named for Judge Elmer Dundy (Standing Bear case).
county_metadata.research_tips: Dundy County (formed 1873, seat Benkelman) in southwestern Nebraska was carved from unorganized territory. Records are at the courthouse in Benkelman. Dundy County was named for Judge Elmer Dundy, who decided the Standing Bear case. Settlement came through homesteading in the 1880s-1890s, driven by the Burlington Railroad. BLM GLO records document homestead entries. The Republican River valley influenced agricultural development. German, Scandinavian, and Anglo-American settlers predominated. FamilySearch has limited microfilm. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The 1885 state census may have early enumeration. Benkelman's newspapers are useful local sources. This sparsely populated county requires researchers to check neighboring Chase, Hitchcock, and Hayes county records for related families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Dundy County. County Register of Deeds in Benkelman holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Fillmore County formed 1856 from unorganized territory. County seat is Geneva. South-central Nebraska. Geneva Indian School (1884-1934) and Nebraska Girls Industrial School.
county_metadata.research_tips: Fillmore County (formed 1856, seat Geneva) records are at the courthouse in Geneva. The Fillmore County Historical Society preserves local materials. Geneva was the site of the Geneva Indian School (1884-1934) and the Nebraska Girls Industrial School -- institutional records may be at History Nebraska or NARA. Homestead entries at BLM GLO document original settlers. German, Swedish, and Anglo-American settlers predominated. The county's location in the Blue River valley supported agriculture. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The territorial censuses and 1885 state census are available. History Nebraska holds supplementary records. The Geneva Signal newspaper from the 1870s provides local genealogical information.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Fillmore County. County Register of Deeds in Geneva holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.
